summary: Charity Majors argues that individual engineering productivity cannot be meaningfully measured through metrics, as creative professional work is too complex to reduce to discrete measurements. She advocates instead for outcome-based management, team-level health metrics, well-calibrated managers with technical judgment, and DORA metrics as proxies for team effectiveness.
key_points: Individual productivity metrics for engineers are gameable and counterproductive
The team is the irreducible unit of ownership, not the individual engineer
Managers must be technical to cultivate independent judgment about contributions
DORA metrics track team-level engineering productivity effectively
Outcome-based management requires trusting engineers with autonomy
money_quotes: To the extent you can reduce a job to a set of metrics, that job can be automated away.
The team is the irreducible unit of ownership.

It is condescending as fuck for leadership to treat engineers like task-crunching interchangeable cogs. It reveals a deep misunderstanding of how sociotechnical systems are developed and sustained (plus authoritarian tendencies, and usually a big dollop of personal insecurity).
Some of the hardest and most impactful engineering work will be all but invisible on any set of individual metrics.
It is in those gaps where we feel trusted to act that we find joy and autonomy in our labor, where we do our best work as skilled artisans.
key_insight: Engineering productivity should be evaluated at the team level through outcomes and DORA metrics, not through individual metrics that incentivize gaming and ignore the most impactful work.
